gender.female: You ask the property manager about access to the EV charger records. They're more forthcoming than their counterpart at the desk.

EV Chargers.default: Multiple charging points and multiple timestamps. The lot logs every vehicle that plugged in. So do you.

gender.male: You photograph the charger unit IDs and cross-reference them with the access log.
gender.female: You ask the attendant which charger was in use last night and at what time. They remember because it blocked a bay.

EV Charging.default: Stationary vehicle, measurable window, logged session. Someone parked here for a reason that had nothing to do with the battery.

gender.male: You compare the charging duration against the vehicle's stated capacity. The math doesn't add up.
gender.female: You check the session history on the public terminal. The user forgot to sign out.

EV Charging Station.default: Infrastructure for the future with the accountability of the past. Every charge session creates a record.

gender.male: You note the vehicles currently charging and the ones that finished recently. One didn't leave.
gender.female: You ask the building attendant for the charging log as part of a routine fleet check. They produce it.

Electric Car Charging.default: Quiet vehicles and traceable sessions. The silence is its own kind of cover. The log is yours.

gender.male: You pull the session data. Three entries in the last week at hours that suggest surveillance, not commuting.
gender.female: You verify the session timing against the incident. The vehicle was here when it shouldn't have been.

Electric Vehicle Charging.default: Modern infrastructure with old-fashioned evidence. Whoever used this station left a trail they didn't think about.

gender.male: You trace the account back through the app registration. The name at the end of the chain is interesting.
gender.female: You flag the session anomaly to the facility manager. Their surprise at the timing confirms the significance.

Elevator.default: A box that logs its trips. Every floor visited, every time the doors opened. The elevator doesn't forget who rode it.

gender.male: You check the service panel. The elevator visited the restricted floor three times last night without a recorded purpose.
gender.female: You ride to the top and work down. The floor that smells different from the rest is the one you stop at.

Elevators.default: Multiple cars and multiple witnesses who forgot they were witnesses. The lobby camera captures everyone who rides.

gender.male: You review the arrival timestamps for the relevant floor. The sequence is wrong for a legitimate visit.
gender.female: You ask the building manager about elevator access logs. They're more detailed than the policy brochure suggests.

Emergency Call Box.default: A fixed point of contact in an area the building considers high-risk. Its placement tells you what the building was worried about.

gender.male: You check whether the call box was activated recently. The indicator says yes. No report was filed.
gender.female: You press the test button and note the response time. Seven minutes is too long for a building this size to not have a reason.

Emergency Call System.default: Panic buttons and response protocols. A system like this keeps logs. One of those logs shows an activation that never became an incident.

gender.male: You pull the activation history. One event was manually cleared before a response was dispatched.
gender.female: You ask the security supervisor about the suppressed alert from three days ago. They consult their notes before answering.

Emergency Exits.default: The way out that nobody uses until they have to. And the way in that nobody watches as carefully as the front door.

gender.male: You test the bar on the exterior side. The alarm's been disabled. Recently.
gender.female: You examine the door frame for contact marks. Someone's been using this exit regularly and not as an emergency.

Emergency Lighting.default: Backup illumination for when the main power fails. Someone wanted to make sure they could still see — or still be seen — when everything else went dark.

gender.male: You check the battery charge on the emergency panels. Fresh. Tested recently. Anticipation or routine?
gender.female: You note which areas the emergency lighting covers and which it deliberately doesn't. The gap is informative.

Emergency Room.default: The end of someone's very bad night and the beginning of your investigation. Whatever came through those doors, it matters.

gender.male: You present credentials to the triage nurse and ask about a specific arrival time. The nurse checks. The record exists.
gender.female: You speak with the attending physician directly. Physicians respond to directness. You get the clinical summary.

Employee Break Area.default: Off the floor and off the record. The break area is where the real opinion of the workplace gets expressed.

gender.male: You arrive with a coffee and nothing to prove. The room tells you everything it's been holding.
gender.female: You sit down and ask how long people have been here. The longest-serving person in the room knows everything.

Employee Break Room.default: Vending machines and the unguarded conversation of people who've temporarily stopped performing their professional role.

gender.male: You leave the door open. Break rooms with closed doors generate less conversation. You want conversation.
gender.female: You ask where the nearest printer is as an excuse to be in the room. The excuse doesn't matter. The presence does.

Employee Cafeteria.default: The same faces every day, the same tables, the same hierarchies playing out over subsidised food. The seating pattern is as revealing as anything in the HR file.

gender.male: You take the table nobody sits at and watch the ones everybody does.
gender.female: You join an occupied table and introduce yourself. The group decides you're alright before the main course.

Employee Store.default: Internal retail with staff-only access. What the employee store stocks tells you what the workforce is being managed with.

gender.male: You use a visitor lanyard to get past the door. The store hasn't been stocked in two weeks. That matters.
gender.female: You ask a staff member to help you find the store. On the way there, the staff member explains why they haven't been going.

Engineering Office.default: Blueprints and specifications and the technical memory of every system in the building. The answer to what's wrong with this place is probably in here.

gender.male: You ask to see the current building schematic under the pretext of a facilities review.
gender.female: You enquire about a specific system discrepancy. The engineer pulls the design specs without hesitation and walks you through the anomaly themselves.

Entertainment Suite.default: A private room designed for pleasure and the off-the-record conversations that happen inside it. What gets said in an entertainment suite stays there — unless you were in it.

gender.male: You get the suite number from the concierge and arrive at exactly the right moment.
gender.female: You enter with the composure of someone who was expected. Nobody corrects that assumption.

Entertainment Zone.default: An area dedicated to distraction. The louder and more distracting the zone, the better the cover for what's happening on the edges of it.

gender.male: You position yourself at the boundary of the zone. What comes in and goes out matters more than what happens inside.
gender.female: You move through it with purpose. Purposeful movement in a distraction zone makes you effectively invisible.

Equipment Storage.default: Everything the building uses to function, locked away and catalogued. One item on the shelf isn't on the catalogue.

gender.male: You check the cage inventory against the issue log. The discrepancy is two items and it's not a counting error.
gender.female: You ask the stores manager about the last non-standard draw. They tell you the date without needing to check.

Escalators.default: Moving between floors in public view. Nobody watching escalators is watching carefully. You are the exception.

gender.male: You ride up and study everyone coming down. The escalator is a controlled sight line. Use it.
gender.female: You stop at the base and look up. The angle from the bottom of the escalator shows you the entire next floor.

Escape Room.default: A puzzle designed with the solution already known. You've been working rooms where nobody knew the solution for years.

gender.male: You solve it in eighteen minutes and use the remaining time to study the monitoring setup.
gender.female: You let the group lead and observe what each person does when they feel stuck. Pressure reveals method.

Espresso Machine.default: High-pressure extraction and the ritual of a drink that takes two minutes to make and generates ten minutes of standing around. Useful.

gender.male: You make your shot and stay by the machine. Everyone comes to the machine eventually.
gender.female: You offer to make one for someone nearby. The offer generates a conversation that the coffee extends.

Event Control Center.default: The operational brain behind anything with a crowd. Every camera feed, every radio channel, every incident comes through here.

gender.male: You enter with the bearing of a duty supervisor. Nobody questions that in a room running on adrenaline.
gender.female: You identify the senior controller and speak directly to them. Senior controllers respond to brevity and precision. You have both.

Event Space.default: Neutral ground built for occasions. What occasion was held here recently that nobody put on the public calendar?

gender.male: You check the booking register under a facilities query. The event three nights ago has a different name in the system than the one you were told.
gender.female: You speak to the venue coordinator about catering logistics. Catering details include guest counts and specific names.

Evidence Room.default: Chain of custody and the locked record of every case the building has touched. Something in here shouldn't be here, or something that should be here isn't.

gender.male: You review the access log. Three sign-outs in forty-eight hours for a case that's been closed for six months.
gender.female: You request a specific exhibit from the log. The supervisor checks and comes back with a different item number. That's the discrepancy.

Exam Rooms.default: Clinical space and clinical conversation. The examination is where the body tells the truth whether the patient intends it to or not.

gender.male: You check which rooms were occupied during the relevant window. Three were booked. One wasn't in the appointment system.
gender.female: You speak to the charge nurse about the intake from Tuesday evening. The nurse consults the chart and the expression changes.

Executive Floors.default: The building above the building. Where decisions happen that the floors below implement without knowing the full picture.

gender.male: You badge through to the executive floor with the confidence of a man who's been there before. You haven't. Doesn't matter.
gender.female: You take the executive elevator with a folder under your arm and a destination that doesn't include the lobby.

Executive Lounge.default: Premium comfort and the honest conversation of people who've decided the rules don't apply to them in a room like this.

gender.male: You take the armchair in the corner and project the ease of someone who belongs. The room accepts the projection.
gender.female: You're approached within four minutes. The room assumes you're there for a specific reason. You are, just not the one they're imagining.

Executive Suite.default: The room at the top. Whoever occupies this suite makes decisions that affect everyone below. You want access to the decision record.

gender.male: You arrive with a scheduled conflict that requires the suite to be cleared. It clears.
gender.female: You're sent up with a message that doesn't require a response. You take thirty seconds of the suite's information before you leave.

Family Restrooms.default: Private, single-occupancy, and equipped for more than the standard transaction. These rooms serve purposes their designers didn't intend.

gender.male: You note which family restroom has an occupied indicator that hasn't changed in twenty minutes. Interesting.
gender.female: You check the log for the accessible unit. It was occupied for forty minutes during the relevant window. There are no children registered in the facility.

Family Visiting Room.default: A space designed to make difficult meetings bearable. Whatever was said in this room under those conditions mattered more than anything said elsewhere.

gender.male: You review the visitation log for the week. One visit wasn't entered until the following day.
gender.female: You speak to the coordinator about the visiting protocol. The deviation from that protocol is what you're following.

Fellowship Hall.default: The community's social infrastructure. Everything the building means to the people who use it gets expressed in this hall.

gender.male: You arrive at the end of a gathering and stay while people are leaving. The conversation in the last ten minutes of any meeting is the real meeting.
gender.female: You volunteer to help with the setup. People who set up the hall know everything about what happened at the last one.

Finance Department.default: Numbers that tell the building's true story. The finance department has the real version of every official account.

gender.male: You request a reconciliation report on a specific cost centre. The CFO's office sends someone down who wasn't supposed to get involved.
gender.female: You ask the finance officer about a line-item anomaly. The officer explains it and in the explanation creates a new problem for their employer.

Finance Office.default: The ledger room. Where the building's financial decisions get recorded and the creative accounting lives between the entries.

gender.male: You present an audit query under a letterhead that carries weight. The office opens the books.
gender.female: You ask about a specific payment authorisation. The office manager pulls the file and the authorisation signature is someone who shouldn't have had that access.

Financial Aid.default: Applications and assessments and the intimate financial disclosure of people trying to get ahead. One of those files has a different kind of value.

gender.male: You request information about the institutional process. The process description includes a specific workflow gap.
gender.female: You present yourself as a case reviewer. Financial aid offices respond to reviewers because reviewers can close the funding.

Fine Dining.default: The most expensive cover story available. In fine dining, every transaction is noted and every guest is remembered.

gender.male: You dine slowly and let the sommelier fill the evening's gaps with conversation.
gender.female: You're seated at the best table without requesting it. The sommelier decides you're worth the attention. So do you.

Finished Goods Area.default: Product ready to ship. This is where what the factory made becomes what the invoice claims. The discrepancy between the two is your case.

gender.male: You count the pallets and compare against the production log. The count is low by exactly enough to be non-accidental.
gender.female: You photograph the lot numbers on the finished goods and cross-reference them against the shipping manifest. Three lots have no destination.

Fire Escape.default: The emergency exit that becomes the quiet entrance. Fire escapes exist to be used in a crisis. You define crisis broadly.

gender.male: You take the stairs from the outside. The door at the third floor is held with a wedge. That's not the fire code.
gender.female: You note which fire escape has fresh marks on the latch. Someone's been using it for access, not egress.

Fire Lane.default: A clear path that legally can't be blocked. Which means it's always clear for the vehicle that needs to get out fast.

gender.male: You note the vehicle parked in the fire lane with expired plates. It's been here since before the incident.
gender.female: You photograph the fire lane from two angles. The camera mounted on the building doesn't cover the lane. It was positioned that way.

Fire Pit.default: Controlled combustion and the social ritual that happens around an open flame. Things get said around fire pits that wouldn't get said anywhere else.

gender.male: You take a position on the far side of the fire and listen to the near side.
gender.female: You introduce yourself to the group around the fire. Fire circles are intimate. Intimacy generates honesty.

Fire Safety System.default: Sensors and alarms calibrated to detect the specific kind of heat and smoke produced by an accident. Not the kind produced on purpose.

gender.male: You review the system log. One sensor was manually disabled during the window in question.
gender.female: You ask the facilities manager about the last system test. The last test result had an exception that was never explained.

Fire Suppression.default: Automated chemical response to heat signatures. If this system activated, there's a record. If it didn't when it should have, there's also a record.

gender.male: You check the suppression log. The system saw the heat but didn't respond. Overridden.
gender.female: You identify which zone was isolated from the suppression network. It was isolated three hours before the event.

Fireplace.default: Heat and light and the ease that open flame creates in a room. People relax by a fire. Relaxed people tell the truth.

gender.male: You take the armchair nearest the fire and let the room organise itself around you.
gender.female: You tend the fire briefly and the room thanks you for it. The thanks opens a conversation.

First Aid Station.default: Bandages and basics and the log of every minor incident that the building officially recorded. What's minor in the log may not be minor in context.

gender.male: You read the incident log at the station. One entry from last week describes a wound that doesn't match the stated cause.
gender.female: You enquire about the station's recent use. The volunteer who manages it keeps better records than the official system.

Fitness Center.default: Weights and cardio and the particular honesty of people who've worked hard enough to stop performing.

gender.male: You complete a set at the rack nobody's touching and earn the right to be in the room.
gender.female: You take the machine with the best sight line and stay for as long as the investigation requires.

Fitness Room.default: On-site exercise space and the conversations that happen after the endorphins kick in. Post-workout is the most candid state most people reach at work.

gender.male: You arrive during the lunch-hour rush and end up in the conversation you needed.
gender.female: You use the space and stay visible. Fitness rooms create instant social parity. You use that.

Fitting Rooms.default: Private, curtained, and with a mirror that sees everything in the small space the curtain creates. What happens in fitting rooms stays in fitting rooms. Unless you were listening.

gender.male: You take the fitting room adjacent to the occupied one and listen to the transaction being conducted.
gender.female: You ask the fitting room attendant about a specific customer earlier in the day. The attendant has a clear memory.

Fleet Parking.default: Vehicles assigned to the organisation and the movement log that tracks every one of them. One vehicle wasn't where it was supposed to be.

gender.male: You review the fleet movement log against the trip records. A four-hour discrepancy for unit seven has no paper behind it.
gender.female: You speak to the fleet coordinator about an anomaly in the GPS data. They confirm it before asking how you accessed the GPS data.

Floral Department.default: Fresh flowers and the regular customers who buy them for occasions and obligations. One of those occasions is yours.

gender.male: You buy a single stem and chat with the florist. Florists remember the large orders and the strange ones.
gender.female: You ask about a specific arrangement placed earlier in the week. The florist remembers the name on the card.

Folding Tables.default: Temporary infrastructure for temporary purposes. The folding tables were up for something and somebody knows what.

gender.male: You ask the facilities staff when the tables were last set up and for what. They tell you.
gender.female: You note the wear pattern on the floor where the tables were arranged. The arrangement tells you the approximate number of people.

Food Court.default: Mass catering and the convergence of everyone who has fifteen minutes and nowhere specific to be. Good cover. Good listening.

gender.male: You take a central table and let the noise work for you. The food court carries everything.
gender.female: You move between counters and catch fragments. Fragments, assembled, are a full picture.

Food Hall.default: Curated vendors and the social performance of eating well in public. The food hall conversation is never really about the food.

gender.male: You browse the stalls and let the browsing give you thirty minutes of unquestioned access to the space.
gender.female: You ask a vendor about their busiest hours. The answer places specific people at specific times.

Forklift Area.default: Heavy machinery and the discipline of a workspace where an error is fatal. The people who work here are precise. Which means imprecision stands out.

gender.male: You read the incident board near the forklift bay. One near-miss was reported at an unusual hour.
gender.female: You speak to the lead operator about a reported damage to racking. The damage happened when the shift log says nothing was moving.

Fountain Court.default: A central architectural feature and the natural gathering point of a building with nowhere better to stand. Everyone passes through a fountain court.

gender.male: You take a position near the fountain and let the space deliver its information.
gender.female: You sit at the court edge with your phone and observe. In a fountain court, sitting still makes you invisible.

Free Breakfast.default: Inclusive catering and the loose conversation of people who've been fed before they've had to perform. Early morning candour is the best kind.

gender.male: You sit at the common table. Hotel breakfasts create accidental communities. You join one.
gender.female: You pour coffee for the person next to you. The gesture generates a conversation that runs through to the second cup.

Front Desk.default: The building's official memory. Everyone who enters and exits passes through the front desk's awareness. So does every event that got logged.

gender.male: You sign in under a name that carries the right kind of weight. The desk waves you through.
gender.female: You approach the desk with a specific enquiry and redirect it naturally to the one you actually needed answered.

Front Porch.default: The transitional space between private and public where everything gets said that can't be said inside or shouted from the street.

gender.male: You stop on the path and ask for directions. The porch conversation that follows covers more than you came for.
gender.female: You make the approach from the sidewalk and stay at the gate. Asking from the gate is less threatening than the porch. People answer questions from a safe distance.

Fuel Pumps.default: Petroleum and transaction records. Every fill-up is logged by pump, by time, by card. Someone stopped here at the right time for the wrong reason.

gender.male: You check the station's pump records for the relevant window. One transaction was cash. One cash transaction out of forty.
gender.female: You ask the attendant which pumps have their own cameras versus shared coverage. The answer maps the blind spots.

Fuel Station.default: Fuel in exchange for identity. The card reader knows who stopped here and when, and the system keeps records longer than people expect.

gender.male: You review the forecourt camera timestamps. Two vehicles at the same pump, forty seconds apart. That's not a coincidence.
gender.female: You ask about the fuel log for a specific account number. The manager produces it without a formal request.

Fueling Station.default: Bulk fuel distribution for a fleet or facility. High-volume fueling is tracked precisely because fuel is expensive and theft is easy.

gender.male: You cross-reference the fuel dispensed against the declared mileage. Twelve vehicles are running longer routes than their logs admit.
gender.female: You ask the fuel supervisor about variance on a specific unit. The variance is deliberate. The supervisor knows.

Full Kitchen.default: Commercial cooking capability and the tight social structure of a kitchen crew. The kitchen knows the building's schedule before anyone else does.

gender.male: You enter during prep and look like you're there for a handover. The kitchen absorbs you.
gender.female: You arrive with a delivery confirmation that needs a signature. The chef signs and offers information you didn't ask for.

Funeral Services.default: The formal management of grief and the paperwork that gives legal form to death. Someone in this building's records crossed your case.

gender.male: You request the service record for a specific date. The director checks and mentions a detail they assumed you already knew.
gender.female: You speak with the director with the measured tone that the occasion requires. The director speaks more openly in response.

Game Room.default: Recreation space and the informal governance of the people who use it most. The game room has a social hierarchy and a history.

gender.male: You challenge the current champion. Either they beat you and you've earned their attention, or you beat them and you've earned the room's.
gender.female: You join a game already in progress and bring enough skill to be welcomed. The room opens up over the next thirty minutes.

Garden.default: Private green space where the rhythms of a household play out in view of anyone who takes the time to observe.

gender.male: You find the angle from the lane where the garden is visible. You've been patient before.
gender.female: You offer to help with the weeding. Gardeners talk. You're very willing to listen while your hands are occupied.

Gas Station.default: Petrol, snacks, and the timestamp that puts someone at a specific location at a specific moment. The forecourt camera doesn't lie.

gender.male: You pay cash at the pump and ask the attendant about the vehicle that was here around the time you're interested in.
gender.female: You review the pump receipt dispenser times against your window. One receipt is missing. Cash transaction.

Genealogy Room.default: Family histories and the archival record of who came from where and who's related to whom. A genealogy room can connect two names that don't appear to be connected.

gender.male: You request the records for a specific surname in the relevant location. The connection you find isn't one the family publicises.
gender.female: You ask the genealogy librarian to help you trace a specific line. The librarian's expertise takes you somewhere the direct search wouldn't.

Gift Shop.default: Souvenirs and the particular transaction of people buying things they'll never use. What they buy tells you who they're buying it for.

gender.male: You browse and let the shop assistant assume you need help. You don't. The assumption generates conversation.
gender.female: You ask about a bulk order placed recently. The shop assistant checks the log and describes the buyer.

Gift Wrapping.default: The concealment of content beneath presentation. What's being wrapped, for whom, and by whose account — all of that is recorded.

gender.male: You enquire about a specific order number under the pretext of a pickup. The counter staff checks the system.
gender.female: You request gift wrapping for something small and ask about the service's busiest period. The staff describe a specific client's standing order.

Golf Simulator.default: Synthetic fairways and real conversations. The golf simulator is where deals get done with the plausible deniability of sport.

gender.male: You take a bay and play three holes while the conversation in the next bay completes its first nine.
gender.female: You request the bay adjacent to the one you're interested in. The partition is thin and the conversations carry.

Green Roof.default: Vegetation on top of the building and the unmonitored access that comes with maintenance access. Nobody cameras the green roof.

gender.male: You get to the roof during a maintenance window and use the elevated position for what it's worth.
gender.female: You request a sustainability tour. Green roof tours are a genuine service and they give you the building's highest vantage point.

Green Room.default: Where performers wait to go on. What gets said in a green room before a performance is what people actually mean — the performance hasn't started yet.

gender.male: You enter as part of the crew and are immediately included in the pre-show conversation.
gender.female: You arrive with something that needed to be delivered backstage. The delivery brings you into the room and the room brings you into the conversation.

Grilling Area.default: Open flame and the particular social ease of outdoor cooking. People relax at a grill in ways they don't relax indoors.

gender.male: You offer to take a turn at the grill. Whoever controls the grill controls the conversation.
gender.female: You bring something to contribute and join the group around the grill. The contribution makes you immediately part of the social structure.

Grooming Station.default: Animal hygiene and the owners who watch the process like it matters personally. It does. That attachment is leverage.

gender.male: You ask about the grooming appointment log for a specific client. The groomer checks without questioning why.
gender.female: You book an appointment under a pretext and use the time to observe the facility and the clients waiting.

Group Fitness Studio.default: Collective exertion and the stripped-down honesty of people who've worked too hard to maintain their usual presentation.

gender.male: You join the class and push hard enough to be noticed. Being noticed earns a conversation after.
gender.female: You arrive early and introduce yourself to the instructor. Instructors know their regulars, including the one who stopped coming.

Guest Apartments.default: Short-term accommodation inside the building's perimeter with all the access that implies. Whoever stayed here had more than just a bed.

gender.male: You request the guest apartment log under a service maintenance pretext. The occupancy record tells you who was here and when.
gender.female: You ask the concierge about the last occupant of the unit you're interested in. The concierge remembers.

Guest Lounge.default: A comfortable room for people who are technically here for someone else. What guests observe while waiting is unfiltered by the building's official narrative.

gender.male: You settle into the guest lounge and let the building move around you for twenty minutes.
gender.female: You strike up a conversation with the person who's been waiting longest. They have opinions about what they've seen.

Guest Parking.default: Vehicles registered to visitors. The guest parking log is where everyone who came here and didn't have a permanent space gets recorded.

gender.male: You check the validation stamps. One vehicle was validated six times this month. Nobody visits this building six times in a month casually.
gender.female: You ask the parking attendant about the regular in the reserved guest bay. They have a regular who uses the same bay every Tuesday.

Guest Suite.default: Premium accommodation inside the building, extended-stay capable, and with access that the standard guest floor doesn't have.

gender.male: You book the suite adjacent to the one you need. Walls in this building are thinner than the rate card implies.
gender.female: You request the suite on a corporate account that opens doors the standard rate doesn't. You use every door it opens.

Guest Suites.default: Multiple private units and the movement log that shows who occupied which one and when. One of those occupancies matters.

gender.male: You review the occupancy record for the relevant period. Suite four was occupied under a name that doesn't match any corporate account.
gender.female: You ask the front desk manager about a booking discrepancy. The suite was supposedly empty. The minibar charges say otherwise.

Gymnasium.default: High ceiling, hard floor, the echo of exertion. The gymnasium is where effort gets stripped of pretence.

gender.male: You put in the work and earn the floor's acceptance. Physical credibility opens doors that credentials don't.
gender.female: You use the space with the focused efficiency of someone who isn't here for the exercise. Which is true.

HOA Common Area.default: Shared space governed by bylaws and the unwritten rules of proximity. The HOA common area is where the neighbourhood's politics play out.

gender.male: You show up to the notice board at the right time and catch the conversation about what's been posted.
gender.female: You ask a resident about the recent changes to the common area rules. The conversation covers considerably more than rules.

HOA Office.default: The administrative heart of a residential community with access to resident records, complaint histories, and maintenance logs.

gender.male: You request the complaint log for the last quarter under the pretext of a property query. The log is illuminating.
gender.female: You ask the HOA manager about a specific resident's service requests. The manager is more forthcoming than they realise.

Hair Salon.default: Forty-five minutes of immobilised candour. The chair is where people say everything they've been holding since their last appointment.

gender.male: You make the appointment and listen. The stylist talks. So does the person in the next chair.
gender.female: You're in the chair and the whole salon knows it. The information flows without prompting.

Handicap Spaces.default: Reserved access and the permit that grants it. One vehicle in a handicap space doesn't have a permit. It has a reason.

gender.male: You note the vehicle without a permit. The plate doesn't match any registered accommodation need.
gender.female: You photograph the space and the vehicle. The permit in the windscreen is a reproduction. A good one.

Hazmat Storage.default: Regulated chemicals in a controlled room, logged and limited. What's in here is dangerous. What shouldn't be in here is more dangerous.

gender.male: You check the manifest against the physical inventory. Three chemicals are present that aren't on the manifest.
gender.female: You request the hazmat log from the safety officer. One entry was amended after the fact. The original phrasing was crossed out but still readable.

Health Clinic.default: Routine care and the clinical record of everything routine that turned out not to be.

gender.male: You present a query about occupational health records. The clinic manager responds to occupational health queries with full cooperation.
gender.female: You ask the clinic nurse about the last incident report filed. The nurse describes it before checking whether you have access to it.

Hearing Aid Center.default: Assistive devices and the patients who depend on them. The hearing aid centre keeps exact calibration records tied to specific users.

gender.male: You enquire about a service appointment for a specific device model. The technician checks the serial number log.
gender.female: You ask about the patient who came in for an urgent calibration last week. The technician provides the appointment detail before realising they shouldn't.

Hearing Assistance.default: Accommodation that documents who needed it and when. The record of assistance is more specific than most people assume.

gender.male: You request the accommodation log for the facility during the relevant event. The log has a name you recognise.
gender.female: You ask about the hearing loop installation date and the last event it was activated for. The dates correlate with your timeline.

Helipad.default: A rooftop landing zone that makes a building accessible without going through the front door. Whatever arrived or departed here, it bypassed all standard access points.

gender.male: You get access to the roof under a maintenance pretext and check the pad surface. Recent touchdown. Very recent.
gender.female: You review the building's air traffic log via the relevant authority. One unlogged landing, twelve days ago.

Holding Cells.default: Temporary containment and the particular candour of people who've been separated from their lawyer for thirty minutes.

gender.male: You enter the cell block under official cover and use the five minutes of access carefully.
gender.female: You speak through the door in the quiet tone that's less threatening than rank. The occupant answers more questions.

Home Medical Equipment.default: Clinical-grade equipment in a non-clinical setting. What's in this building that requires hospital-level support?

gender.male: You check the equipment against the prescription on file. The equipment is over-specified for the stated condition.
gender.female: You speak with the care coordinator about the equipment inventory. The inventory reveals a second user the case file doesn't mention.

Home Office Nook.default: Private work within a shared living space. The nook is where the building's resident conducts business they don't discuss at the table.

gender.male: You note the monitor angle and what it reveals during a casual visit. What's on the screen has nothing to do with household accounts.
gender.female: You ask to use the space briefly. The desk surface tells you more than the resident intended.

Hose Tower.default: Elevated equipment storage and a view over the station's operational footprint. The hose tower is the highest point with the best sight lines.

gender.male: You climb the tower during a training drill and use the elevation.
gender.female: You ask to observe from the tower as part of a facility assessment. The captain agrees. You use every minute of it.

Host Stand.default: The first point of contact and the reservation record that names everyone who sat in this restaurant tonight.

gender.male: You arrive without a reservation and speak to the host with the unhurried confidence of someone who always gets a table.
gender.female: You ask the host about a specific table booking from earlier in the week. The host checks the book and confirms the party size.

Hot Desks.default: Unassigned workstations where the previous occupant always leaves something behind — usually something they didn't mean to.

gender.male: You claim a desk near the charging bar and note the browser tab left open on the adjacent screen.
gender.female: You set up at a desk that was clearly used this morning. The notepad impression under the blotter is legible.

Hot Food Bar.default: Prepared food and the social ease of a shared meal option. People make decisions quickly at a hot food bar. Sometimes they say things just as quickly.

gender.male: You take a position at the end of the bar where the queue forms and the conversations happen.
gender.female: You ask the server about the day's preparation. Servers describe their day. The day has a detail in it.

Hot Food Counter.default: A service counter where brief transactions and briefer conversations add up to a complete picture of the morning.

gender.male: You order and wait. The wait at a hot food counter is never silent.
gender.female: You compliment the service and ask how long they've been on. The server answers and mentions who was in earlier.

Human Resources.default: Personnel records and the institutional memory of every complaint, review, and termination. The HR file on the person you're looking for is more complete than their CV.

gender.male: You request records under an employee relations review. HR cooperates with reviews because the alternative is worse.
gender.female: You present your query as a welfare concern. HR is more responsive to welfare concerns than formal requests.

ICU.default: Intensive care and the critical period where the most important facts about a person are written in their physiology. The chart tells you what happened even if the patient can't.

gender.male: You speak to the attending with the directness of someone who needs the clinical picture. They provide it.
gender.female: You identify the lead nurse and introduce yourself briefly. Nurses control access to information in the ICU more than anyone else.

IMAX Screen.default: Maximum immersion and minimum awareness of anything outside the frame. The IMAX screen puts a thousand people in the same mental state simultaneously. You're the only one watching the audience.

gender.male: You take the back row centre and spend the film watching what the film was designed to prevent you from watching.
gender.female: You find the seat with the best peripheral view and spend two hours in professional observation.

IT Department.default: The nervous system of the building's digital life. Every email, every access log, every flagged file exists somewhere in the IT department's records.

gender.male: You submit a help-desk ticket that gives you a reason to be in the department. Once you're in the department, you're in.
gender.female: You speak to the IT manager about a network anomaly you've prepared in advance. The anomaly is real enough to discuss. The discussion covers what you came for.

IV Therapy.default: Intravenous administration logged to patient, substance, and time. What was administered here, and under what authorisation, tells a story.

gender.male: You review the administration record for the relevant window. One IV order was verbal, not written. That's a protocol deviation.
gender.female: You ask the duty nurse about the patient's treatment plan. The nurse describes the IV protocol and notes the deviation themselves.

Ice Machine.default: The one appliance in any building that people visit at all hours without thinking about it. The hallway outside an ice machine is the best casual surveillance point in the building.

gender.male: You take the corridor outside the ice machine and look like you're waiting for someone. Everyone comes to the ice machine eventually.
gender.female: You fill your bucket and step back into the corridor. The ice machine sees more foot traffic than the lobby at two in the morning.

Ice Rink Equipment.default: Specialised machinery and the maintenance schedule that keeps it running. The rink has off-hours that aren't in the public calendar.

gender.male: You ask about the maintenance window schedule. The ice is resurfaced twice a night. The second resurface is when the rink is empty.
gender.female: You request a tour of the equipment room under a facilities pretext. The operator explains the off-hours schedule without being asked.

Ice Skating Rink.default: Open ice and exposure. Nowhere to hide on a rink, which means the people who choose to be seen here are choosing deliberately.

gender.male: You skate the perimeter twice and use the circuit to cover the whole venue.
gender.female: You rent skates and take to the ice. A woman on a rink alone gets watched. You watch back.

Imam's Office.default: Private counsel and the weight of a community's trust. Whatever the Imam was told in confidence, the fact that they were told matters.

gender.male: You knock with respect and ask to speak privately. The Imam receives you. The conversation that follows is careful and honest on both sides.
gender.female: You ask for guidance on a matter that connects to the case. The guidance includes context the Imam hadn't realised they were sharing.

Indoor Seating.default: Tables and chairs where transactions of every kind take place and where proximity creates the conditions for conversation you weren't meant to hear.

gender.male: You take the table against the wall with a line of sight to the rest of the room.
gender.female: You sit in the centre of the room. Centre tables hear everything. Most people avoid them for exactly that reason.

Infant Care.default: The most closely supervised part of any facility because the stakes are absolute. The staff here notice everything.

gender.male: You present credentials and request to speak to the charge nurse. The charge nurse is the person who actually knows what happened.
gender.female: You introduce yourself with the appropriate weight and speak directly. The charge nurse responds to directness in a way they don't respond to rank.

Infinity Pool.default: The edge of the building and the visual statement of a facility that wants you to believe the world stops where it does. It doesn't.

gender.male: You take the far end of the pool and observe the building's rooftop access points from the water.
gender.female: You're at the pool alone before the morning crowd arrives. The poolside attendant talks freely to someone at this hour.

Information Booth.default: The official answer to questions the building is willing to answer. What they deflect from the information booth is as useful as what they provide.

gender.male: You ask a question you already know the answer to. The deflection tells you what you needed.
gender.female: You ask three routine questions and one that isn't. The information booth answers the first three and hesitates on the fourth. The hesitation is your answer.

Innovation Lab.default: The space where the building's future gets developed and the present gets covered up. Innovation labs are poorly secured because security is seen as the opposite of innovation.

gender.male: You enter during a session and look like you're affiliated with the current project. Nobody checks affiliations in an innovation lab.
gender.female: You introduce yourself as a researcher and ask what they're working on. Innovators explain what they're working on to anyone who shows genuine interest.

Insurance Services.default: Policies and claims and the paper trail of everything that was insured and everything that happened to it. The claim history is the case history.

gender.male: You present a query about a policy number. The adjuster pulls the file without asking for authorisation.
gender.female: You ask about a claim filed during the relevant period. The adjuster describes the claim before confirming your access level.

Intercom System.default: Controlled entry and the recorded request at the door. Every buzz of an intercom is a logged contact between the outside and the inside.

gender.male: You ring the intercom and use the thirty-second response window to observe the street. The response tells you who's in.
gender.female: You ring and state your name with the assurance of someone who was expected. The door opens.

Interrogation Room.default: One table, two chairs, a mirror that's only one-way from one side, and the deliberate architecture of pressure. You've been on both sides of this room.

gender.male: You take the chair that faces the door. Old habit.
gender.female: You sit in the chair that doesn't face the mirror. People talk more honestly when they're not performing for an audience they can't see.

Interview Room.default: A contained space for controlled conversation. What gets said in this room is on the record. What doesn't get said is on your record.

gender.male: You arrange the chairs slightly asymmetrically before they arrive. Small physical discomfort generates informational discomfort.
gender.female: You adjust the room to make it feel less like an interview room before you begin. People answer questions more fully when they don't feel questioned.

Inventory System.default: The digital ledger of everything the building has and where it is. When the inventory lies, it lies consistently, which makes the lie visible.

gender.male: You run the variance report. Three product categories have a pattern that a random system error doesn't produce.
gender.female: You ask the inventory manager to walk you through the reconciliation process. In the walkthrough, they create the evidence themselves.

Isolation Ward.default: Containment and the particular privacy of a space designed to keep things in and people out. Something in here doesn't need to be contained for medical reasons.

gender.male: You request access under an inspection protocol. The ward supervisor checks your authorisation level and grants it.
gender.female: You speak to the ward nurse with the precision of someone who knows exactly what they're looking for. The nurse responds with the same precision.

Judge's Chambers.default: Private judicial space where the law gets decided before the courtroom announces it. The chambers are where the real arguments live.

gender.male: You wait in the clerk's anteroom and listen to what the clerk assumes you can't hear through the door.
gender.female: You request a meeting through the clerk with the stated purpose that the judge will find harder to decline than approve.

Juice Bar.default: Health food and the candid morning conversation of people who came here to feel virtuous. They talk freely when they feel virtuous.

gender.male: You order something green and unpleasant and take the counter stool. The regular next to you fills the silence.
gender.female: You ask the staff about their busiest hour. The description includes a regular who isn't there anymore.

Jumbotron.default: A screen designed to make sixty thousand people look in the same direction. You're looking at the sixty thousand.

gender.male: You position yourself below the Jumbotron. The crowd watching it isn't watching the ground level.
gender.female: You use the Jumbotron's reflective panel during the dark transitions. It shows you the section behind you.

Jury Assembly.default: Citizens waiting to perform a civic function they mostly didn't volunteer for. The jury assembly room is the most candid room in the courthouse.

gender.male: You take the rear row and let the room's anxiety work on everyone else.
gender.female: You join the group conversation that forms naturally while people wait. Two hours of waiting produces remarkable candour.

Kennels.default: Animals in temporary housing and the staff who manage them. The kennel log has a name attached to every animal. One of those names matters.

gender.male: You ask about a specific animal described by breed and circumstance. The kennel worker checks the intake log.
gender.female: You enquire about an animal brought in under unusual circumstances. The worker remembers because unusual stays with you.

Kids Area.default: A dedicated space that draws families and the particular oversight of parents watching their children instead of anything else. You're watching something else.

gender.male: You position yourself at the boundary of the kids area with the unremarkable patience of a waiting parent.
gender.female: You help a child who can't reach something and earn an easy introduction to the parent. Parents talk to people who are kind to their children.

Kids Play Area.default: Controlled chaos and the divided attention of adults managing it. The play area is the best place in any building to be unremarkable.

gender.male: You take the adult bench and look like you belong to a child somewhere in the area. Nobody checks.
gender.female: You engage with the activity briefly and earn the social acceptance of the other parents. Social acceptance grants you twenty minutes of unquestioned presence.

Kids Zone.default: A branded entertainment space that exists to occupy children while their parents do other things. What the parents are doing while the kids are occupied is worth knowing.

gender.male: You observe who drops their children and stays close and who uses the zone to be somewhere else.
gender.female: You help with the check-in process and get a full view of the parent-child pairs coming through.

Kiss and Ride.default: The transit facility designed for brief handoffs. Vehicles stop, exchange something or someone, and leave. Nobody watches the kiss and ride closely enough.

gender.male: You sit at the terminal bench and note every vehicle that stops longer than a drop-off requires.
gender.female: You observe from the platform above. The kiss and ride from above reveals the pattern a ground-level view hides.

Kitchen.default: The engine room of any building that serves food. The kitchen knows the building's schedule before anyone else and talks about it freely.

gender.male: You enter through the service door and look like you belong on the line. The kitchen is too focused to question you.
gender.female: You arrive with a question about the kitchen's hours and get a full tour of the operation.

Lab.default: Scientific process and the records that document it. What was tested in this lab, and what those tests found, is what you're here for.

gender.male: You request access under a quality assurance review. Labs cooperate with QA because QA can shut them down.
gender.female: You speak directly to the lead researcher. Researchers respond to specific, well-framed questions. You have one.

Lab Area.default: Open bench space and the collaborative nature of scientific work. Lab areas are harder to compartmentalise than private labs. Which means more of it is visible to you.

gender.male: You observe from the doorway and note which work areas have been recently cleared. Cleared benches mean something was recently completed.
gender.female: You enter with a credentialed escort and use the escort's access to cover more ground than you'd manage alone.

Lab Services.default: The administrative interface between the lab and the outside world. Sample requests, result reporting, the paperwork that surrounds clinical science.

gender.male: You present a sample query with just enough technical detail to be credible. The lab services coordinator pulls the request history.
gender.female: You ask about turnaround times and probe the system's capacity for tracking specific sample origins. The coordinator explains more than the question required.

Laboratory.default: Controlled environment and the formal documentation of what happened in it. Every procedure in a laboratory leaves a record. You're reading the record.

gender.male: You review the log book near the entrance. The last three entries are in a different handwriting. The regular researcher hasn't been in.
gender.female: You speak to the research technician and ask about the most recent anomalous result. The tech explains it and hands you the supporting data without being asked.

Landscaping.default: Managed vegetation and the outdoor perimeter of a building's identity. What grows where, and how it's maintained, tells you about the building's blind spots.

gender.male: You walk the perimeter under the cover of inspecting the grounds. The camera gaps are between the second and third trees on the east side.
gender.female: You speak to the groundskeeper as you pass. Groundskeepers observe the building from outside for hours every week. What they've seen is worth asking about.

Laser Tag.default: Dark spaces and objective-based movement. Laser tag venues were built for the tactical mind. You're using the darkness for a different objective.

gender.male: You play the game and use the arena to cover the facility's layout. You were mapping the space before the briefing ended.
gender.female: You take the elevated position in the third zone and use it for what elevated positions are for.

Laundry.default: Industrial washing and the methodical removal of what things were carrying. What was washed here recently mattered enough to clean quickly.

gender.male: You check the laundry log for rush items processed outside of normal hours.
gender.female: You ask the laundry supervisor about any non-standard processing in the last week. The supervisor describes one item that stood out.

Laundry Room.default: Machines and the people who use them at strange hours. The laundry room at two in the morning attracts a specific kind of person.

gender.male: You sit with a book you're not reading and wait for the room to tell you something. It always does.
gender.female: You load a machine and sit down. Shared laundry spaces generate the kind of conversation that happens between strangers at two in the morning.

Law Library.default: Casebooks and precedents and the research that tells you what the law says before the court decides what it means. You've spent time in these stacks.

gender.male: You pull the relevant case file and find the annotation someone left that wasn't intended to be found.
gender.female: You ask the librarian for the most recent citations to a specific statute. The access log for those citations has three names in it.

League Office.default: Sports administration and the institutional memory of every game, every player, and every transaction that kept the season running.

gender.male: You present as a compliance officer. Sports administration is perpetually nervous about compliance.
gender.female: You request a meeting with the league director about a scheduling matter. The meeting gives you access to the administrative floor.

Lecture Halls.default: Tiered seating and the one-to-many transfer of information. The hall is built for authority. What gets said from the front shapes what everyone in the seats believes.

gender.male: You take a seat in the back row. The back row hears everything and is heard by nobody.
gender.female: You sit at the side, third row from the front. The angle gives you both the speaker and the audience reaction.

Library.default: Stacks and silence and the institutional promise that information should be free. Most information is. Some isn't. You're here for the kind that isn't.

gender.male: You pull the volume that nobody else has touched and find the margin note that was meant to stay private.
gender.female: You ask the reference librarian for help with a specific research question. The librarian's expertise turns two days of work into forty minutes.

Library Lounge.default: The library's social space, where the strict silence rule relaxes enough for conversation. Those conversations accumulate.

gender.male: You take the armchair nearest the reading group and let the discussion come to you.
gender.female: You join the group with a question about what they're discussing. You came prepared to ask it.

Lift Equipment.default: The mechanical room that nobody visits unless they're authorised or determined. The lift equipment room has access to every floor in the building.

gender.male: You access the machine room under a service pretext. The maintenance log tells you which floors have been visited and when.
gender.female: You identify the service technician on the way out and ask a question about the recent service history. They answer in full.

Light Show.default: Spectacle designed to overwhelm the senses. Under the light show, everything happening in the room below it is effectively invisible.

gender.male: You move through the venue during the light show. Nobody's watching ground level.
gender.female: You position yourself at the control point. The person who operates the show sees the entire venue from one chair.

Lighting.default: The way a space presents itself after dark. What the lighting reveals is intentional. What it hides is more interesting.

gender.male: You map the shadows before you need to use them. Good habits.
gender.female: You assess the lighting from the entrance. Three cameras are positioned in the lit zones. The unlit zones are yours.

Living Quarters.default: Where the building's residents sleep, eat and drop their guard. The living quarters tell you more about someone than their office does.

gender.male: You observe what's visible without entering. Enough of a picture to understand what you're dealing with.
gender.female: You read the arrangement of a person's living space as fluently as a case file. It tells you the same things.

Loading Area.default: The back entrance to everything. Loading areas exist to move things without announcement. That lack of announcement is a gap in the record.

gender.male: You walk the loading area with a clipboard and the body language of someone checking a manifest. Nobody stops you.
gender.female: You ask the receiving clerk about deliveries during the relevant window. The clerk checks without questioning your authority.

Loading Dock.default: Pallets and movement and the chain of custody that begins at the dock. Where did the dock log show a shipment going that never arrived?

gender.male: You review the dock log for the day in question. One inbound is logged without a corresponding outbound disposition.
gender.female: You approach the dock supervisor with a discrepancy you identified. The supervisor explains it and in explaining creates a new problem.

Loading Docks.default: Multiple bays and the full picture of what a facility moves in a day. One bay handles something that doesn't appear on the manifest.

gender.male: You compare the bay allocation log against the vehicle movement records. Bay three wasn't assigned anything. Bay three was in use.
gender.female: You ask the dock manager to walk you through the current allocation. The explanation accounts for four of the five bays.

Loan Office.default: Credit and the paper trail of who borrowed what under what terms and what they did with it.

gender.male: You enquire about a specific account under an authorisation that technically covers it. The officer checks and confirms the credit history.
gender.female: You sit down with the loan officer and present a query about a specific lending pattern. The officer describes it before asking why you want to know.

Lobby.default: The building's public face and its first filter. Everyone who enters this building passes through the lobby. You have.

gender.male: You cross the lobby with the pace of someone arriving for a meeting. Nobody interrupts a man with that pace.
gender.female: You take a seat in the lobby seating and observe the full space for four minutes before you move. You know everything you need to.

Local History Archive.default: The neighbourhood's paper memory going back further than anyone living remembers. The answer to what happened here before it was what it is now.

gender.male: You request the files from the decade that matters. The archivist finds the box without hesitation.
gender.female: You ask the archivist to help you find a connection between two addresses across a thirty-year gap. The connection exists.

Locker Room.default: The space between performance and identity. The locker room is where people say what they actually mean after they've stopped performing.

gender.male: You enter during the quiet period between sessions. The room tells you things when it doesn't know anyone's listening.
gender.female: You speak with the attendant about the facility's security procedures. The attendant describes a recent breach that wasn't in the report.

Locker Rooms.default: Multiple secure areas and the combination of privacy and shared space that makes people less careful than they think.

gender.male: You check which lockers have been assigned this week versus which have been in continuous use. The continuous-use ones belong to people who didn't want to take things home.
gender.female: You ask the facility manager about access logs for the locker rooms during a specific window. The manager provides them.

Lost and Found.default: Everything people didn't care enough to come back for. Or couldn't. The lost and found is an inventory of people's last known movements.

gender.male: You describe the item you're looking for without explaining why it matters. The attendant checks every box.
gender.female: You ask whether a specific type of item was turned in recently. The description you give is precise enough that the attendant recognises it immediately.

Lottery Terminal.default: Small stakes and the paper evidence of hope. The lottery terminal log places specific people in specific locations at specific times.

gender.male: You ask the cashier about the terminal's transaction history for a specific date. They recall one customer who was there unusually long.
gender.female: You purchase a ticket and ask about the terminal's busiest hours. The cashier tells you and mentions the regular who was there the night of the incident.

Lounge Area.default: Transitional seating where nobody's committed to being anywhere. The lounge area is occupied by people waiting for things and saying more than they intend.

gender.male: You take the corner seat with sight lines to all entrances. Then you wait. Waiting is the job.
gender.female: You sit in the middle of the lounge. Midroom placement means you're part of every nearby conversation without initiating any of them.

Lounge Bar.default: Softer lighting and slower drinking and the conversations that require both before they happen.

gender.male: You order and occupy. A man drinking slowly at a lounge bar is the furniture. Furniture hears everything.
gender.female: You're approached within ten minutes. You redirect the approach into the conversation you came for.

Luggage Storage.default: Bags checked and tagged. The luggage storage log places people in the building at the time they left their bags. One person left their bags and never came back.

gender.male: You describe the bag and ask if it was collected. It wasn't. The tag date tells you everything.
gender.female: You present a claim ticket for a bag you didn't check. The attendant checks the tag number in the system and the result answers your question.

Detailing Bay.default: The bay smells of wax and chemicals. Someone worked hard to make something look clean. You wonder what they were covering up.

gender.male: You've run plates in worse places. The detailing bay is slick floors and chemical shine — the kind of clean that hides a multitude of sins.
gender.female: The bay reeks of solvent and effort. You've learned that the more someone polishes, the more they're trying to erase.

Detective Office.default: A room that's seen too many secrets. Stale coffee, old case files, and the faint scent of cigarettes that nobody admits to smoking.

gender.male: Your office. Organized chaos only you understand. The photos on the board connect dots nobody else bothered to look for.
gender.female: Your office. Half the precinct thinks it's a mess. You know exactly where every piece of evidence is, and that's what counts.

Detergent Dispenser.default: A wall-mounted dispenser, half empty. Whoever uses this place cleans up their messes on a schedule. Or tries to.

gender.male: Functional. Impersonal. Tells you this place processes a lot of dirty laundry — the literal kind, at least.
gender.female: You note the soap level. Someone refills it regularly. Someone's paying attention to the small things around here.

Diagnostic Station.default: A terminal of blinking readouts and cable bundles. Whatever runs through here, someone's watching every number it produces.

gender.male: You scan the readouts. Data doesn't lie — people just misread it on purpose. You won't.
gender.female: The screens show vitals, error codes, system states. You read diagnostics like other people read faces — same skill, different subject.

Digital Menu Boards.default: Bright screens cycling through prices and specials. Everything looks better lit up. Everything looks worse in the morning.

gender.male: The boards flip to a lunch special. You haven't eaten. The case doesn't care.
gender.female: The menus glow above the counter. You note the prices have gone up since last month. Everything has.

Digital Signage.default: Screens everywhere, pushing messages at you. In this city, information is currency and everyone's selling.

gender.male: The displays cycle through ads and alerts. You've learned to read between the pixels.
gender.female: Digital signs blink their bright lies. You've been trained to look past the message to the messenger.

Imaging.default: A room of machines that see through skin and bone. Everything looks different when you strip away the surface.

gender.male: The imaging suite hums quietly. Whatever it finds in there, you'll deal with it the same way you deal with everything — straight on.
gender.female: The machines here reveal what's hidden. You respect that. You've spent a career doing the same thing.

Luxury Suites.default: Suites reserved for those who can afford to be comfortable while everyone else isn't. Thick walls. Thicker secrets.

gender.male: Luxury suites — the kind of rooms where deals get made that never appear in any record. You've cracked a few of those deals before.
gender.female: The suites up here are polished to a mirror sheen. The higher the floor, the less they expect someone like you to come knocking.

Luxury Wing.default: A corridor where the carpet is thick enough to muffle footsteps and conversations alike. Somebody paid for that privacy.

gender.male: The luxury wing. You walk it slowly. Expensive silence has a way of hiding expensive problems.
gender.female: You move through the wing carefully. Places this quiet aren't peaceful — they're controlled. Someone controls them.

Mail Center.default: Rows of boxes and a sorting table. Every package that passes through here has a story. Some of those stories are crimes.

gender.male: You've traced more than one lead through a mail center. Paper trails are old-fashioned. They're also reliable.
gender.female: The mail center hums with small deliveries and big implications. You know how to read a return address.

Mail Room.default: Packages stacked, envelopes sorted. The overlooked nerve center of any operation. Nobody notices the mail room until something goes missing.

gender.male: The mail room — underpaid, underestimated, and the first place you check when things disappear.
gender.female: You've solved cases that started in rooms exactly like this. What gets delivered tells you everything about what people want.

Mail Service.default: A service desk for packages and correspondence. Everything sent and received passes someone's hands. Those hands remember.

gender.male: You lean on the counter. The clerk has seen enough to write a novel. You just need one chapter.
gender.female: You present your badge. Mail service workers see more than anyone gives them credit for. That works in your favor.

Mailboxes.default: A wall of small locked doors. Everyone has a box. Everyone has something they're waiting to receive or hoping won't arrive.

gender.male: You scan the names on the boxes. One of them is lying to you already. You just don't know which yet.
gender.female: The mailboxes stand in orderly rows. You've learned to read the names like a roster of suspects.

Maintenance Bays.default: Wide open bays for heavy work. The kind of place where sweat and grease are honest currencies and nobody asks too many questions.

gender.male: The bays smell like engine oil and hard work. You know both well enough.
gender.female: You walk the maintenance bays. The workers size you up. You let them. You're sizing them up right back.

Maintenance Room.default: Mops, chemicals, and the skeleton keys of buildings. The maintenance room knows every floor intimately. So does the person who works it.

gender.male: The maintenance room is a map of the building's hidden bones. You've found more answers behind these doors than in most offices.
gender.female: You push the door open. The maintenance room smells like bleach and utility. It also smells like access.

Maintenance Shop.default: Tools on every surface and a work order board covered in jobs. Somebody keeps this place running. That somebody sees everything.

gender.male: The shop is organized the way a careful mind works — everything in its place, everything for a reason.
gender.female: You step into the maintenance shop. The supervisor looks up with a measuring gaze. You return it with interest.

Maker Space.default: A workshop of ideas and half-finished things. Ingenuity lives here, alongside the occasional violation of building safety codes.

gender.male: The maker space is cluttered with ambition. Someone here knows how to build things. You wonder what else they've built.
gender.female: You move through the maker space. Creative minds, scattered projects. You note who's working late and what they're working on.

Management Office.default: The room where decisions get made and blame gets redistributed. The desk is important. The person sitting at it thinks so too.

gender.male: You knock before entering the management office. The occupant already knows you're trouble. That's fine. So do you.
gender.female: The management office door is half open. You push it the rest of the way. Whoever runs this place is about to have a worse day.

Massage Chair.default: A chair designed to unknot human tension. You note it's been used recently. Hard work or hard stress — either way, someone needed it.

gender.male: You glance at the massage chair. Not now. Maybe never, on this job.
gender.female: The massage chair hums invitingly. You file the thought away for after the case closes — if it closes.

Maternity Ward.default: A ward of new beginnings, sterile and careful. Life arriving under fluorescent lights. Even here, you've seen stories that break your heart.

gender.male: You keep your voice low in the maternity ward. Whatever you're investigating, it doesn't belong in a room where people are just starting.
gender.female: The maternity ward is quiet except for the small, insistent sounds of new life. You do your job and get out fast. Some places deserve peace.

Mayor Office.default: The mayor's office. Power, public trust, and plenty of reasons to wonder which one is real.

gender.male: You've been in the mayor's office before. The handshakes are warm, the eyes are cold, and the agenda is always somewhere else.
gender.female: You enter the mayor's office with your badge and your patience. Politicians wear their smiles like armor. You know how to get past it.

Meat Department.default: Cold cases and hanging cuts. The department operates under bright lights and sharp knives. You feel at home.

gender.male: The meat department is cold and blunt. No frills. You respect that.
gender.female: You pass through the meat department quickly. The chill is real, the lighting is harsh, and the workers have seen sharper things than you.

Media Booth.default: A glass box of lenses and broadcasting equipment. Whoever sits in there controls what the public sees. That's a dangerous power.

gender.male: You glance up at the media booth. The camera never lies — but the person controlling it decides what it points at.
gender.female: The media booth looks down on everything. You make a mental note: everything said within range of those microphones is on record.

Mediation Rooms.default: Rooms designed for compromise. You've seen what happens when compromise fails. That's usually when you get called in.

gender.male: The mediation rooms are quiet and neutral. Don't let that fool you — the silence in here has broken people.
gender.female: You step into the mediation room. Disputes walked in here and left as settlements. Or grievances. Sometimes both.

Medical Clinic.default: A clinic humming with hurried purpose. People come here with pain and leave with answers — or more questions.

gender.male: You've visited enough medical clinics to know: the waiting room tells you more than the diagnosis ever will.
gender.female: The clinic smells of antiseptic and anxiety. You read the faces in the waiting area. One of them isn't here for their health.

Medical Office.default: A doctor's office — certificates on the wall, files locked away, and a degree in keeping secrets that the diploma never mentions.

gender.male: You scan the medical office. Credentials displayed, patient files locked. You have a warrant and a reason.
gender.female: The medical office is organized and guarded. Getting information out of a doctor takes patience and a subpoena. You have both.

Medical Records.default: A room full of what people never wanted to tell their doctors but eventually had to. Every folder is a confession.

gender.male: Medical records — the truth written in clinical language. You read between the diagnoses.
gender.female: You approach the records desk. Every file in here is someone's private history. You only need one.

Medical Room.default: A room prepared for emergencies. Clean, ready, and carrying the quiet weight of every person who has lain on that table.

gender.male: The medical room is ready for whatever comes next. So are you.
gender.female: You note the equipment in the medical room. Prepared for crisis. You've learned to live in that readiness.

Medical Storage.default: Shelves of supplies under lock and key. What's stored here can heal or harm depending on the hands that reach for it.

gender.male: Medical storage — controlled, catalogued, and occasionally a motive.
gender.female: You check the lock on the storage cabinet. Everything in here is accounted for. Something always gets unaccounted for eventually.

Medication Storage.default: A secure room of compounds and controlled substances. The inventory is tight. It has to be — because the demand never stops.

gender.male: You stand in the medication storage room. A lot of people would risk a lot to get their hands on what's in these cabinets.
gender.female: Every vial and bottle is logged. You check the log. A detective knows that the gap between what's recorded and what's present is where crime lives.

Meet-Me Room.default: A small room of cables and cross-connects. The place where networks talk to each other. Everything digital passes through here eventually.

gender.male: The meet-me room smells like routers and cold air. Data moves through here in the dark, the way things always do.
gender.female: You stand in the meet-me room. If you could read network traffic the way you read people, this room would tell you everything.

Meeting Room.default: Four walls, a table, and whatever version of truth people decide to bring with them.

gender.male: You've sat on both sides of a meeting room table. Today you're running the meeting.
gender.female: The meeting room is set for a conversation that nobody in it wants to have. That's the kind you're best at.

Meeting Rooms.default: A cluster of conference rooms where decisions are made, deals are struck, and cover stories are rehearsed.

gender.male: Meeting rooms — the city's busiest theater. Every door hides a different performance.
gender.female: You walk past the meeting rooms. Murmured conversations, closed blinds. You make note of which rooms are in use.

Meeting Space.default: A blank canvas of chairs and whiteboards. It becomes whatever the people who use it need it to be. Including a problem.

gender.male: The meeting space is neutral ground. You've negotiated in worse places.
gender.female: You survey the meeting space. Whoever reserved this room left behind more than they intended to.

Meeting Suites.default: Upscale conference rooms with catering and privacy glass. The premium option for conversations people really don't want overheard.

gender.male: The meeting suites are designed for discretion. You've learned that premium privacy usually means premium secrets.
gender.female: You check the booking log for the suites. Whoever met here paid extra not to be seen. You'll find out why.
